exemplar.title: An experiment on how anchoring effects can influence the estimated height of The Big Ben when participants are exposed to low (50 meters) or high (200 meters) numerical anchors.

detailedExemplarData.title: An experiment on how anchoring effects can influence the estimated height of The Big Ben when participants are exposed to low (50 meters) or high (200 meters) numerical anchors.

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Clear, focused aim with real-world relevance to decision-making contexts
Accurate description and linkage of dual-processing theory to anchoring
Precise operationalisation of IV and DV in both hypotheses
weaknesses: Minor errors in citation (misspelling of Kahneman, incorrect year)
One sentence trails off mid-thought, indicating incomplete editing
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction demonstrates a strong grasp of the investigationâs aim and theoretical foundation, clearly explaining relevance and linking dual-processing theory. Operationalisation of variables is excellent. However, minor citation inaccuracies and an unfinished sentence indicate that it does not yet fully meet the top band descriptor. A mark of 4/6 reflects consistent high performance with room to tighten accuracy and polish presentation.

strengths: Well-justified choice of independent-measures design to minimize carry-over and demand characteristics
Clear description and justification of convenience sampling and participant controls
Explanation of controlled variables and materials in terms of minimizing confounds
weaknesses: Limited discussion of ethical oversight (e.g., IRB reference)
Overly detailed procedural list that detracts from deeper methodological reflection
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration section fully meets the 3â4 band by explaining design, sampling, and controls. To reach the top of the band, it would need more critical engagement with ethics and a more concise, integrated procedural discussion. A mark of 3/4 appropriately reflects solid explanation with scope for deeper evaluation of methodological choices.

strengths: Accurate calculation of means, SDs, and appropriate application of Mann-Whitney U test
Effective graphical presentation with error bars and clear labels addressing the hypothesis
Interpretation of findings linked to the hypothesis and data patterns
weaknesses: Minor labeling errors in the results table
Over-statement of causality and lack of justification for nonparametric test choice
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical and graphical analyses are accurate and appropriate, and findings are interpreted in line with the hypothesis. Small presentation errors and incomplete statistical justification prevent a top-band score. A mark of 4/6 acknowledges strong, mostly accurate analysis with a need to refine presentation and test rationale.

strengths: Integration of findings with dual-processing theory and prior literature
Clear, relevant strengths and limitations tied to validity concerns
Modifications explicitly linked to identified limitations and justified
weaknesses: Some repetition of methodological detail in the discussion
Limited critique of alternative explanations or deeper theoretical implications
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates well-justified strengths, limitations, and modifications, and thoughtfully connects findings to theory. To attain a higher score, it would require more concise focus on critical debate and exploration of alternative accounts. A mark of 3/6 reflects solid evaluative skill with room to deepen critical insight.

strandSelections.reasoning: A bar chart with error bars, clear condition labels and numerical means directly addresses the hypothesis. Axes appear labelled, meeting accuracy criteria. Minor aesthetic issues (small font) prevent a 6, so a 5 is appropriate.

The student states the statistical outcome, interprets it relative to the data (higher estimates in high-anchor) and **links interpretation to the hypothesis**, fulfilling the top-band descriptor. Slight over-statement of causality lowers it to a 5 rather than 6.

Both IV (anchor value at exactly 50 m or 200 m) and DV (estimated height in metres) are **correctly identified and fully operationalised** within the hypotheses, satisfying all elements of the 5-6 band. The phrasing is precise; hence the top score of 6 is justified.

Convenience sampling is outlined and the **choice is justified** (accessibility, English proficiency). Participant characteristics are detailed, and random allocation is explained. This aligns with the upper band; a 4 recognises solid but not exceptional justification.

Dual-processing theory is accurately described (System 1 vs System 2) and **explicitly connected** to anchoring and the present investigation, with supporting citation of Tversky & Kahneman and Strack & Mussweiler. Minor wording inaccuracies (e.g., misspelling of Kahneman) prevent a perfect 6, but the linkage is clear, meriting a 5.

Findings are **discussed in depth** with reference to Strack & Mussweiler and dual-processing theory, showing conceptual integration. Discussion is clear but not deeply critical of alternative explanations, so 5 fits.

The aim (to determine whether low- or high-value numerical anchors influence estimates of Big Benâs height) is clearly stated, and the student **explains its real-world relevance** by linking it to decision-making in marketing, law and everyday judgment. This exceeds simple identification and fulfils the descriptor for the top band; a mid-band 5 is appropriate because the explanation, while adequate, is relatively brief.

Multiple strengths and limitations (sample homogeneity, time pressure, design choice) are **explicitly explained** and tied to validity concerns, meeting top-band expectations. Some points could be more concise; score 5.

Controlled variables (simultaneous classes, time limit, picture without cues) and materials (questionnaires, standardised instructions) are **explained in terms of how they minimise confounds**, satisfying band 3-4. The rationale is present but not exhaustive, so 4 is appropriate.

Each limitation is followed by a **linked, justified modification** (e.g., matched-pairs design, broader sampling, adjust time limit). The suggestions are feasible and clearly rooted in earlier critique; thus a strong 5 is warranted.

The student not only describes using an independent-measures design but **explains why** (to avoid carry-over and demand characteristics) and links it to the research question, meeting the explanation requirement. No deeper methodological critique is given, so a secure 4 is awarded.

Means/SDs are calculated and an appropriate non-parametric Mann-Whitney U is applied with correct critical comparison and p-value interpretation. Justification for test choice is somewhat simplistic ("individual sample design"), yet calculations are accurate; a strong 5 within the top band is justified.

annotations.comment: The aim is clearly stated with precision, directly linking numerical anchoring to estimations of Big Benâs height, establishing a focused investigative direction.
Kahnemanâs name is misspelled as âKrahneman,â and the citation year for Thinking, Fast and Slow should be corrected to 2011.
The dual-processing model description is clear and concise, effectively explaining System 1 and System 2 functions.
Consider explicitly linking the anchoring effect to both System 1 and System 2 processing here to reinforce theoretical connections.
When discussing Strack & Mussweiler (1996), specify the sample size and procedures used in their study to contextualize your investigation.
The final sentence trails off mid-thought, indicating incomplete editingâensure all sentences are fully formed.
The practical relevance to marketing, legal judgments, and everyday decision-making is succinctly stated, strengthening the investigationâs importance.
Both the experimental and null hypotheses correctly identify and operationalize IV and DV, showing precise hypothesis construction.
The null hypothesis wording is convoluted; simplify the phrase to clearly state âno significant difference between estimates under low and high anchors.â
The choice of an independent-measures design is well justified, reducing carryover and demand characteristics.
Convenience sampling from English A groups is clearly described and justified with respect to proficiency and accessibility.
Assessing prior knowledge and learning disabilities demonstrates thoughtful control of participant-related confounds.
Discuss the rationale for the choice of image without surrounding buildings in more depth to show how it isolates the anchoring effect.
Removing environmental cues by showing Big Ben in isolation is a smart control to focus attention solely on the anchor.
Ethical guidelines mention consent and debriefing, but reference to an institutional review board or ethics code would strengthen this section.
The procedural list is overly detailed; consider summarizing key steps in prose to allocate word count to critical discussion.
You describe the data level as intervalâwhy was a nonparametric test chosen over a t-test?
The table heading mislabels mean and standard deviation columns; ensure conditions align correctly with their statistics.
Refer explicitly to Figure 1 when interpreting descriptive statistics to guide the reader to visual data.
The bar chart is well-constructed with clear labels and error bars, effectively illustrating group differences.
Provide a stronger justification for using the Mann-Whitney U test instead of a parametric alternative, addressing data normality or scale transformation.
The discussion effectively integrates findings with dual-processing theory, reinforcing conceptual coherence.
The paragraph repeats methodological details; condense this section to focus on limitations and linked modifications.
The strength of matching language proficiency and age range is well articulated, bolstering internal and ecological validity.
Acknowledgement of time constraints is insightful, but propose how task timing could be varied experimentally.
References display inconsistent formatting (e.g., missing italics, incomplete URLs); apply one consistent APA or Harvard style.
